question: how to improve soil microbiome
title: How to Improve Soil Microbiome: Feed the Biology, Not the Plant | Scotty's Gardening Lab
metaDescription: Improving your soil microbiome starts with understanding what kills it — then doing the opposite. Compost, cover crops, and no-till are the foundation. Here's the biology and the practice.
content:
Y'all, I want you to think about the ground under your feet differently. Not as dirt. Not as a substrate you dump fertilizer into and expect to produce food. Think of it as a digestive system — a living, breathing network of organisms that processes organic matter, cycles nutrients, and delivers food to your plants the way your gut delivers nutrition to you.

When Gabe Brown writes in his book *Dirt to Soil* that synthetic fertilizers damage the microbial and fungal communities in the ground — that they tend to burn them a little bit, actually — he's describing something real that every regenerative grower has observed firsthand. The fertilizer performs in year one. The soil biology doesn't recover quickly. And by year five, you need more fertilizer because you've killed the organisms that were doing the work for free.

I've been out in Needville, Texas trying to reinvigorate dead, compacted land. You can see land like that — it hasn't got the life in it. No microbes, no earthworms, no crickets. Nothing. It's gray and dense and repels water. And the path back from dead soil to living soil is not a synthetic one. It goes through biology.

**The short answer:** To improve your soil microbiome, you need to feed it organic matter, stop disturbing it with tillage, stop poisoning it with synthetic inputs, and introduce the organisms you want to cultivate through compost and mycorrhizal inoculants. That's the framework. The details are about how you sequence those steps for your specific soil conditions.

## What Actually Lives in Healthy Soil

Before we talk about improvement, let's get honest about what we're trying to build. Because if you don't understand what healthy soil microbiome looks like, you can't work toward it with any real intention.

A teaspoon of biologically active garden soil contains up to one billion bacteria. Several yards of fungal filaments. Several thousand protozoa. Scores of nematodes. All of that in a teaspoon. That's not metaphor — that's measurement, documented by the USDA's Soil Biology Primer.

At the base of the soil food web you have bacteria and fungi. They're the primary decomposers. They break down organic matter — dead plant material, wood chips, compost — and convert it into forms that plant roots can absorb. Bacteria are specialists in nitrogen cycling. Fungi are specialists in phosphorus uptake and in physically connecting plant roots to mineral resources across distances the roots themselves could never reach.

Above bacteria and fungi in the food web, you have protozoa and nematodes eating bacteria and fungi. Above those, you have arthropods, beetles, ants, and earthworms eating the smaller organisms. And all of this feeding and dying and decomposing produces plant-available nutrients at every step in the cycle.

This is what Albert Howard observed in Indian traditional farming. The life in the soil — the full web, from bacteria through earthworms — is the mechanism of fertility. You don't create fertility by adding chemicals. You create it by supporting biology.

## The Things That Kill Soil Biology (Stop Doing These First)

Improving a soil microbiome is as much about what you stop as what you start. Most damaged soil microbiomes got that way through a combination of the same mistakes.

**Tillage** physically destroys fungal hyphal networks. Mycorrhizal fungi grow their network of threads — hyphae — through soil over months and years. A single pass of a rototiller shreds that network. Every time you till, you restart the fungal community from near-zero. Gabe Brown's no-till approach is built around this observation: leave the fungal network intact, and the soil biology can compound on itself season after season.

**Synthetic fertilizers** suppress microbial activity by providing nutrients in a form so concentrated that plants take them up without the help of microbial intermediaries. When plants don't need the microbes to deliver nutrients, they stop feeding the microbes. Mycorrhizal fungi in particular are suppressed by high-phosphorus fertilizers because the plant stops trading carbohydrates for fungal phosphorus when phosphorus is already abundant in the soil solution. You've short-circuited the relationship.

**Bare soil** is a microbiome killer that people overlook. Soil exposed to direct sunlight and temperature extremes desiccates the surface layers where the highest microbial density lives. Soil needs cover — living plants, mulch, or both — to maintain the moisture and temperature stability that microbes need.

**Pesticides and herbicides** kill selectively in theory but broadly in practice. Glyphosate, for example, has documented negative effects on several beneficial soil bacterial genera. Once you start reading the research on pesticide impacts on soil biology, you can't look at a bottle of weed killer the same way.

## Compost: The Foundation of Microbiome Restoration

If you take one thing from this entire article, let it be this: compost is not fertilizer. Compost is a microbial inoculant. When you apply good, mature compost to your soil, you are introducing hundreds of species of bacteria, fungi, and other organisms that colonize neighboring soil particles and rebuild the food web from the ground up.

Research published in MDPI's Agronomy journal documents how microbial communities in the composting process — and the resulting compost — have measurable positive impacts on soil biota when applied. The active proliferation of fungal and bacterial cells inside well-colonized compost particles spreads to neighboring aggregates, physically expanding the living zone.

In my own practice, I use a hot composting system. I collect organic waste from a local restaurant — onion skins, carrot tops, vegetable trimmings — and combine it with wood chips from tree services. The wood chips slow things down and add the carbon balance. The restaurant scraps provide the nitrogen kick that heats the pile. After a few months of turning and cooking, the result is dark, crumbly, biologically rich compost that smells like a forest floor.

That smell is important. Good compost smells earthy and alive. It smells like the decay cycle in operation. If it smells like rot or ammonia, something went wrong. But when it smells right, you're looking at a community of organisms ready to colonize your beds.

## Cover Crops: Feeding the Web Between Seasons

The soil microbiome needs to eat year-round. In bare soil between growing seasons, that food source disappears and the microbial population crashes. Cover crops prevent that crash.

Plant roots exude carbohydrates — sugars and proteins — into the immediate zone of soil around the root. This exudate is the primary food source for the bacteria and fungi that colonize the rhizosphere, the thin zone of soil immediately adjacent to roots. When you grow a cover crop during the off-season, you maintain that exudate flow. The microbial community stays fed. Population levels stay up. When your cash crop goes in, the biology is ready to support it.

Cover crops also improve soil structure through their root systems, which create channels and aggregates that persist after the plants are terminated. They add organic matter when they break down. Leguminous cover crops like clover and vetch fix atmospheric nitrogen, feeding it into the soil food web through a biological process that fertilizer bags can't replicate.

I run cover crops in all my beds when they're not in production. It's not complicated. It's letting biology fill the space you'd otherwise leave empty.

## Mycorrhizal Inoculants: Jump-Starting the Fungal Network

In severely depleted soil — like the compacted land I was working with in Needville — the native mycorrhizal fungi population may be too low to effectively colonize new plant roots. In that case, inoculation makes sense.

Mycorrhizal inoculants are available as dry powders you apply directly to root zones at planting. The research from Frontiers in Microbiology on mycorrhizal networks in ecological restoration shows that inoculated sites develop more robust plant-microbe partnerships faster than non-inoculated sites, particularly in disturbed soils.

The mechanism is simple. Plant roots release chemical signals that attract mycorrhizal fungi. The fungi colonize the root, forming the physical connection. In exchange for carbohydrates from the plant, the fungal network delivers phosphorus, micronutrients, and water from distances the root could never reach. It's a trade. Both parties benefit. And it's a relationship that, once established, compounds on itself season after season.

The key is not to undermine the relationship with synthetic phosphorus fertilizers once it's established. The plant-fungus trade only happens when the plant actually needs the fungal delivery service. Give the plant everything it needs synthetically and the mycorrhizal network becomes redundant — and eventually disappears.

## Practical Sequence for Rebuilding a Damaged Microbiome

Here's the sequence I'd recommend for someone starting with compacted, depleted urban soil.

**Year one:** Apply three to four inches of good compost to the surface without tilling it in. Let earthworms and soil organisms pull it down. Plant a winter cover crop. In spring, terminate the cover crop by cutting it at the base and laying it flat — don't pull or till. Add another inch of compost on top. Plant your first crops with mycorrhizal inoculant at the root zone.

**Year two:** You'll see aggregate formation beginning. The soil will be a little bit less compacted. Earthworm populations will be visibly higher. Continue the compost-and-cover-crop cycle. Avoid all tillage. Add wood chip mulch paths between beds.

**Year three:** The soil should be noticeably different. Darker, more crumbly, with visible aggregates — those irregular lumps of soil particles glued together by microbial exudates. That's the sign of living soil. That's what you're working toward.

The decay cycle is not a metaphor. It's a real, observable, measurable process happening in your soil right now — or waiting to happen if you give it the inputs it needs. Feed the biology and the biology will feed your plants. That's been true since the first farmer, and it's still true now.

---

4. Frontiers in Microbiology — Roles of Mycorrhizal Symbioses in Ecological Restoration: https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/microbiology/articles/10.3389/fmicb.2025.1456041/full

scottyQuotes: When you add synthetic fertilizers to the ground, we all kind of know that damages the microbial and fungal communities. It tends to burn them a little bit. And when you damage those communities, the plants have to work harder and you get diminishing returns over time.

There is a life web within the soil. It starts with bacteria and fungi and all these very small organisms that are living within the soil, processing all the different calories and minerals and vitamins to provide to the plants.

A lot of times with dead compacted land, you see that it hasn't got the life in it — the microbes and the earthworms and the crickets and all the other things that come with good healthy soil. What we're trying to do is reinvigorate that because it'll fluff the soil up, create pockets where moisture can get absorbed, where roots have an easier time going in.
